company.name: D-Link Systems, Inc.
company.address: 17595 Mt. Herrmann St., Fountain Valley, California 92708
company.business_description: D-Link Systems, Inc. (DLS) is a subsidiary of D-Link Corporation that designs, develops, markets, and distributes networking devices including consumer routers and Internet-protocol (IP) cameras, along with related software and services for the United States market.

case_info.legal_authority: Section 5(a) of the Federal Trade Commission Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a)

complaint.factual_background: D-Link Corporation and its U.S. subsidiary D-Link Systems, Inc. failed to take reasonable steps to secure the software in their consumer routers and IP cameras against well-known vulnerabilities, including hard-coded credentials, command injection flaws, and backdoors. D-Link also exposed its private code-signing key on a public website for approximately six months, and stored users' mobile app login credentials in clear, readable text. Despite these security failures, Defendants marketed their products with prominent claims that the devices were secure, easy to secure, and protected against unauthorized access. Thousands of consumers' devices were left vulnerable to remote attackers who could redirect web traffic, steal financial information, hijack cameras to spy on households, or enlist devices into botnets.

representations_made.description: DLS's Security Event Response Policy stated that D-Link prohibits any intentional product features or behaviors allowing unauthorized access, including undocumented credentials, backdoors, and covert communication channels, and treats all such features as the highest priority
representations_made.quoted_text: D-Link prohibits at all times, including during product development by D-Link or its affiliates, any intentional product features or behaviors which allow unauthorized access to the device or network, including but not limited to undocumented account credentials, covert communication channels, ‘backdoors’ or undocumented traffic diversion. All such features and behaviors are considered serious and will be given the highest priority.

representations_made.description: Router promotional brochures claimed the routers support the latest wireless security features to help prevent unauthorized access and utilize dual active firewalls to prevent potential attacks from across the Internet
representations_made.quoted_text: the router: supports the latest wireless security features to help prevent unauthorized access, be it from over a wireless network or from the Internet. Support for WPATM and WPA2TM standards ensure that you will be able to use the best possible encryption, regardless of your client devices. In addition [the router] utilizes dual active firewalls (SPI and NAT) to prevent potential attacks from across the Internet. Delivering great wireless performance, network security and coverage [the router] is ideal for upgrading your existing wireless network. (See PX 2).

representations_made.description: Router promotional brochures claimed the router ensures a secure Wi-Fi network through WPA/WPA2 encryption and utilizes dual-active firewalls to prevent potential attacks and intrusions from across the Internet
representations_made.quoted_text: SECURITY,” that the router: ensures a secure Wi-Fi network through the use of WPA/WPA2 wireless encryption. Simply press the WPS button to quickly establish a secure connection to new devices. The [router] also utilizes dual-active firewalls (SPI and NAT) to prevent potential attacks and intrusions from across the Internet. (See PX 3).

representations_made.description: Router promotional brochures claimed the router supports the latest wireless security features to help prevent unauthorized access and uses SPI firewalls to help prevent potential attacks from across the Internet
representations_made.quoted_text: The [router] also utilizes dual-active firewalls 16 (SPI and NAT) to prevent potential attacks and intrusions from across the 17 Internet. (See PX 3). 18 c. Under a bolded heading, “Advanced Network Security,” that the router: supports the latest wireless security features to help prevent unauthorized access, be it from over a wireless network or from the Internet. Support for WPATM and WPA2TM standards ensure that you will be able to use the best possible encryption method. In addition, this [router] utilizes Stateful Packet Inspection Firewalls (SPI) to help prevent potential attacks from across the Internet. (See PX 4).

representations_made.description: Router promotional brochures claimed the router protects the network with 128-bit AES encryption and is 'one of the safest' routers available
representations_made.quoted_text: In addition, this [router] utilizes Stateful Packet 23 Inspection Firewalls (SPI) to help prevent potential attacks from across the 24 Internet. (See PX 4). 25 d. Under a heading “128-bit Security Encryption,” that the router: protects your network with 128-bit AES data security encryption – the same technology used in E-commerce or online banking. Create your own network COMPLAINT PAGE name and password or put it at the tip of your fingers with ‘Push Button Security’ standard on every Amplifi device. With hassle-free plug and play installation, and advanced Wi-Fi protected setup, the [router] is not only one of the fastest routers available, its [sic] also one of the safest. (See PX 5).

representations_made.description: IP camera promotional materials displayed the word 'SECURITY' prominently and used the term 'secure connection' next to a lock icon among product features
representations_made.quoted_text: 22. Defendants highlight the security of their IP cameras in a wide range of materials available on Defendant DLS’s website, including user manuals and promotional brochures, which describe these features alongside language that specifically references the device’s “security”. Such materials include, but are not limited to, brochures in the general form of Exhibit 6, which display the word “SECURITY” in large, capital letters, in a vividly- colored footer across the bottom of each page. (See PX 6). In addition, Defendants have designed their IP camera packaging, including in the general form of Exhibit 7, to display security-related terms. Such terms include the words “secure connection,” next to a lock icon, among the product features listed on the side of the box (see PX 7).

representations_made.description: Defendants' router graphical user interface instructed users to set a password 'to secure your new networking device' and stated it is 'highly recommended' to create a password 'to keep your router secure'
representations_made.quoted_text: In particular, in many 17 instances, to begin using the router, users must access a graphical user interface (hereinafter, 18 "Defendants' router GUI"), in the general form of Exhibits 8 and 9, which includes 19 instructions, such as: 20 a. “To secure your new networking device, please set and verify a password below” (see PX 8); and b. “It is highly recommended that you create a password to keep your router secure.” (See PX 9).

representations_made.description: Defendants' IP camera GUI instructed users to set up an Admin ID and Password 'to secure your camera' and displayed a 'SECURICAM Network' banner with a lock icon
representations_made.quoted_text: In particular, to 26 begin using the camera, in many instances, users must access a GUI (hereinafter "Defendants' 27 IP camera GUI"), in the general form of Exhibits 10 and 11, which include language, such as: 28 COMPLAINT PAGE 9 Case 3:17-cv-00039-JD Document 44 Filed 03/20/17 Page 10 of 14 1 a. instructions to “Set up an Admin ID and Password” or “enter a password” in order “to secure your camera” (see PX 10); and b. security-related banners, including, but not limited to, the words “SECURICAM Network,” alongside a lock icon, across the top of the GUI (see PX 11).

counts.title: Unfair Security Practices
counts.representation: Defendants offered routers and IP cameras to consumers for the purpose of protecting their local networks and accessing sensitive personal information, but failed to take reasonable steps to secure the software for these devices
counts.fact: Defendants' failures to implement reasonable security measures caused or are likely to cause substantial injury to consumers that is not outweighed by countervailing benefits and is not reasonably avoidable by consumers
counts.conclusion: Defendants' acts and practices as described in Paragraphs 15-18 constitute unfair acts or practices in or affecting commerce, in violation of Section 5 of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. §§ 45(a) and 45(n).

counts.title: Security Event Response Policy Misrepresentation
counts.representation: Through the Security Event Response Policy, Defendant DLS represented, directly or indirectly, expressly or by implication, that Defendants took reasonable steps to secure their products from unauthorized access
counts.fact: In truth and in fact, Defendants did not take reasonable steps to secure their products from unauthorized access
counts.conclusion: The making of the representation set forth in Paragraph 31 constitutes a deceptive act or practice, in or affecting commerce in violation of Section 5(a) of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a).

counts.title: Router Promotional Misrepresentations
counts.representation: Through promotional materials described in Paragraph 21, Defendants represented, directly or indirectly, expressly or by implication, that the routers described by these claims were secure from unauthorized access
counts.fact: In truth and in fact, Defendants' routers were not secure from unauthorized access and control
counts.conclusion: The making of the representation set forth in Paragraph 34 constitutes a deceptive act or practice, in or affecting commerce in violation of Section 5(a) of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a).

counts.title: IP Camera Promotional Misrepresentations
counts.representation: Through promotional materials described in Paragraph 22, Defendants represented, directly or indirectly, expressly or by implication, that the IP cameras described by these claims were secure from unauthorized access and control
counts.fact: In truth and in fact, Defendants' IP cameras were not secure from unauthorized access and control
counts.conclusion: The making of the representation set forth in Paragraph 37 constitutes a deceptive act or practice, in or affecting commerce in violation of Section 5(a) of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a).

counts.title: Router GUI Misrepresentations
counts.representation: Through the router graphical user interface described in Paragraph 23, Defendants represented, directly or indirectly, expressly or by implication, that the routers described by these claims were secure from unauthorized access
counts.fact: In truth and in fact, Defendants' routers were not secure from unauthorized access and control
counts.conclusion: The making of the representation set forth in Paragraph 40 constitutes a deceptive act or practice, in or affecting commerce in violation of Section 5(a) of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a).

counts.title: IP Camera GUI Misrepresentations
counts.representation: Through the IP camera graphical user interface described in Paragraph 24, Defendants represented, directly or indirectly, expressly or by implication, that the IP cameras described by these claims were secure from unauthorized access and control
counts.fact: In truth and in fact, Defendants' IP cameras were not secure from unauthorized access and control
counts.conclusion: The making of the representation set forth in Paragraph 43 constitutes a deceptive act or practice, in or affecting commerce in violation of Section 5(a) of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. § 45(a).

definitions.Approved Standard: "Approved Standard" shall mean the "Security for industrial automation and control systems – Part 4-1: Secure product development lifecycle requirements", attached hereto as Exhibit A, or, in the event that such standard no longer exists, any successor standard established or approved by the International Electrotechnical Commission, or any successor entity thereto. In the event no such successor standard or successor entity exists, or at the election of Defendant, Approved Standard shall mean a standard of comparable scope and thoroughness approved, at his or her sole discretion, by the Associate Director for Enforcement, Bureau of Consumer Protection, Federal Trade Commission. Any decision not to approve a standard must be accompanied by a writing setting forth in detail the reasons for denying such approval.
definitions.Defendant: "Defendant" means D-Link Systems, Inc. and its successors and assigns.
definitions.Covered Device: "Covered Device" shall mean any IP Camera or Router that Defendant sells on or after January 5, 2017, directly or through authorized re-sellers to consumers in the United States; provided that "Covered Device" does not include IP Cameras or Routers that Defendant can establish that Defendant offers primarily for enterprises and other commercial entities, including products identified in Exhibit B.
definitions.IP Camera: "IP Camera" shall mean any Internet Protocol ("IP") camera, cloud camera, or other Internet-accessible camera that transmits, or allows for the transmission of, video, audio, or audiovisual data over the Internet.
definitions.Router: "Router" shall mean any network device that forwards IP data packets from one network to another or from a network to the Internet.

provisions.title: Comprehensive Software Security Program
provisions.summary: Defendant must establish, implement, and maintain a comprehensive software security program for twenty years designed to protect the security of its Covered Devices, including at minimum nine categories of documented safeguards.

requirements.description: Establish and maintain a comprehensive Software Security Program designed to protect the security of Covered Devices for twenty (20) years after entry of the Order
requirements.quoted_text: IT IS ORDERED that Defendant shall, for a period of twenty (20) years after entry of this Order, continue with or establish and implement, and maintain, a comprehensive software security program (“Software Security Program”) that is designed to provide protection for the security of its Covered Devices, unless Defendant ceases to market, distribute, or sell any Covered Devices. Subject to Section II.I of this Order, to satisfy this requirement, Defendant must, at a minimum:

requirements.description: Document in writing the content, implementation, and maintenance of the Software Security Program
requirements.quoted_text: A. Document in writing the content, implementation, and maintenance of the Software Security Program;

requirements.description: Provide the written program and any evaluations or updates to Defendant's board of directors or governing body at least once every twelve (12) months
requirements.quoted_text: B. Provide the written program and any evaluations thereof or updates thereto to Defendant’s board of directors or governing body or, if no such board or equivalent governing body exists, to a senior officer of Defendant responsible for Defendant’s Software Security Program at least once every twelve (12) months;

requirements.description: Designate a qualified employee or employees to coordinate and be responsible for the Software Security Program
requirements.quoted_text: C. Designate a qualified employee or employees to coordinate and be responsible for the Software Security Program;

requirements.description: Assess and document at least once every twelve (12) months internal and external risks to the security of Covered Devices
requirements.quoted_text: D. Assess and document, at least once every twelve (12) months, internal and external risks to the security of Covered Devices that could result in the unauthorized disclosure, misuse, loss, theft, alteration, destruction, or other compromise of such information input into, stored on or captured with, accessed, or transmitted by a Covered Device;

requirements.description: Design, implement, maintain, and document safeguards as part of a secure software development process, including: security planning, threat modeling, pre-release code review using automated static analysis tools, pre-release vulnerability testing, ongoing code maintenance with a shared-code database, remediation processes, ongoing monitoring of security research, a vulnerability reporting process for security researchers, automatic firmware updates, at least 60-day advance notice to consumers before ceasing security updates, and biennial security training
Assess at least once every twelve (12) months the sufficiency of safeguards in place to address risks to the security of Covered Devices, and modify the Software Security Program based on the results
requirements.quoted_text: F. Assess, at least once every twelve (12) months the sufficiency of any safeguards in place to address the risks to the security of Covered Devices, and modify the Software Security Program based on the results.

requirements.description: Test and monitor the effectiveness of the safeguards at least once every twelve (12) months, and modify the Software Security Program based on the results
requirements.quoted_text: G. Test and monitor the effectiveness of the safeguards at least once every twelve (12) months, and modify the Software Security Program based on the results.

requirements.description: Select and retain service providers capable of maintaining security practices consistent with this Order, and contractually require service providers to implement and maintain such safeguards
requirements.quoted_text: H. Select and retain service providers capable of maintaining security practices consistent with this Order, and contractually require service providers to implement and maintain safeguards consistent with this Order; and

requirements.description: Evaluate and adjust the Software Security Program in light of any changes to Defendant's operations or business arrangements, at a minimum once every twelve (12) months
requirements.quoted_text: I. Evaluate and adjust the Software Security Program in light of any changes to Defendant’s operations or business arrangements, or any other circumstances that Defendant knows or has reason to know may have an impact on the effectiveness of the Software Security Program. At a minimum, Defendant must evaluate the Software Security Program at least once every twelve (12) months and modify the Software Security Program based on the results.

provisions.title: Software Security Assessments by a Third Party
provisions.summary: Defendant must obtain initial and biennial third-party assessments of its Software Security Program or the Approved Standard, conducted by a qualified independent Assessor, with results submitted to the FTC.

requirements.description: Obtain initial and biennial Assessments from a qualified, objective, independent third-party professional who is a Certified Secure Software Lifecycle Professional (CSSLP) with professional experience with secure Internet-accessible devices, uses generally accepted standards, conducts an independent review, and retains all documents for five (5) years

For each Assessment, provide the Associate Director for Enforcement for the Bureau of Consumer Protection at the FTC with the name and affiliation of the person selected to conduct the Assessment, subject to the Associate Director's approval
requirements.quoted_text: B. For each Assessment, Respondent shall provide the Associate Director for Enforcement for the Bureau of Consumer Protection at the Federal Trade Commission with the name and affiliation of the person selected to conduct the Assessment, which the Associate Director shall have the authority to approve in his sole discretion. Any decision not to approve an individual selected to conduct such Assessment must be accompanied by a writing setting forth in detail the reasons for denying such approval.

requirements.description: The initial Assessment must cover from the entry of this Order to January 31, 2020; biennial Assessments must cover each 2-year period thereafter for ten (10) years after entry of this Order
requirements.quoted_text: C. The reporting period for the Assessments to FTC must cover: (1) from the entry of this Order to January 31, 2020, for the initial Assessment; and (2) each 2-year period thereafter for ten (10) years after entry of this Order for the biennial Assessments.

requirements.description: If electing to assess compliance with the Software Security Program, the Assessment must determine whether the program is implemented and maintained, assess the effectiveness of sub-Sections I.A-I, identify gaps or weaknesses, and identify specific evidence examined
If electing to assess compliance with the Approved Standard, the Assessment must certify compliance with the Approved Standard, including specified provisions of IEC 62443-4-1
No Assessment finding may rely solely on assertions or attestations by Defendant's management; the Assessment must be signed by the Assessor stating they conducted an independent review
requirements.quoted_text: F. No finding of any Assessment shall rely solely on assertions or attestations by Defendant’s management. The Assessment shall be signed by the Assessor and shall state that the Assessor conducted an independent review of the Software Security Program or the Approved Standard, and did not rely solely on assertions or attestations by Defendant’s management.

requirements.description: Where third parties implement any Software Security Program criteria, Defendant must provide or cause to be provided to the Assessor all materials and documentation necessary to conduct the Assessment
requirements.quoted_text: G. To the extent that Defendant has selected, appointed, or worked with a third party to implement any of the criteria of the Software Security Program or any criteria of the Approved Standard, Defendant shall provide to the Assessor, or cause to be provided to the Assessor, in connection with the Assessment, all materials and documentation necessary for the Assessor to conduct the Assessment of the effectiveness of the Comprehensive Software Security Program or Approved Standard. All such materials and documentation shall be maintained and produced upon request pursuant to the provisions of this Order.

requirements.description: Each Assessment must be completed within sixty (60) days after the end of the reporting period; the initial Assessment must be submitted to the Commission within twenty (20) days after completion; all subsequent biennial Assessments must be retained and provided to the Associate Director within twenty (20) days of request
requirements.quoted_text: H. Each Assessment must be completed within sixty (60) days after the end of the reporting period to which the Assessment applies. Unless otherwise directed by a Commission representative in writing, Defendant must submit the initial Assessment to the Commission [PROPOSED]
requirements.deadline: 60 days after end of reporting period; submit within 20 days after completion

requirements.description: If Defendant obtains an Assessment certifying compliance with the Approved Standard and with Section I.E.10, Defendant shall be deemed in compliance with Section I for two (2) years from the date of that Assessment or until the next January 31 Assessment deadline, whichever is earlier, subject to exceptions for misrepresentations and material changes in practices
requirements.quoted_text:
I. If Defendant obtains an Assessment (i) certifying that the Software Security Program for the Covered Devices is in compliance with the Approved Standard and (ii) certifying that Defendant is in compliance with Section I.E.10, Defendant shall be deemed in compliance with Section I of this Order for two (2) years from the date of that Assessment or until the next January 31 Assessment deadline, whichever is earlier. Provided, however:

1. Defendant shall not be deemed in compliance with Section I of this Order based on a Section II Assessment if Defendant made a representation, express or implied, that either misrepresented or omitted a material fact and such misrepresentation or omission would likely affect a reasonable Assessor’s decision about whether Defendant complied with the Approved Standard. Further, in the event that such a misrepresentation or omission was made for the purpose of deceiving the Assessor, Defendant shall not be deemed in compliance with any portion of Section I or Section II of this Order based on that Assessment.

2. Defendant shall not be deemed in compliance with Section I of this Order based upon a Section II Assessment if Defendant materially changed its practices after the Assessment in question, unless, at the time of the material change, an Assessor qualified under [PROPOSED]

provisions.title: Cooperation with Third-Party Software Security Assessor
provisions.summary: Defendant must disclose all material facts to the Assessor and provide all necessary information and materials for each Assessment, without misrepresentation.

requirements.description: Disclose all material facts to the Assessor and must not misrepresent in any manner, expressly or by implication, any fact material to the Assessor's Assessment
requirements.quoted_text: A. Disclose all material facts to the Assessor, and must not misrepresent in any manner, expressly or by implication, any fact material to the Assessor’s Assessment; and

requirements.description: Provide or otherwise make available to the Assessor all information and material in its possession, custody, or control that is necessary to the Assessment for which there is no reasonable claim of privilege
requirements.quoted_text: B. Provide or otherwise make available to the Assessor all information and material in its possession, custody, or control that is necessary to the Assessment for which there is no reasonable claim of privilege.

provisions.title: Annual Certification
provisions.summary: Defendant must annually submit to the FTC a sworn certification from a senior corporate manager confirming compliance with the Order and disclosing any material noncompliance.

requirements.description: One year after entry of the Order, and each year thereafter, provide the Commission with a certification from a senior corporate manager or senior officer that: (1) the requirements of this Order have been established, implemented, and maintained; and (2) Defendant is not aware of any material noncompliance that has not been corrected or disclosed to the Commission
requirements.quoted_text: A. One year after the entry of this Order, and each year thereafter, provide the Commission with a certification from a senior corporate manager, or, if no such senior corporate manager exists, a senior officer of Defendant responsible for Defendant’s Software Security Program that: (1) the requirements of this Order have been established, implemented, and maintained; and (2) Defendant is not aware of any material noncompliance that has not been (a) corrected or (b) disclosed to the Commission. The certification must be based on the personal knowledge of the senior corporate manager, senior officer, or subject matter experts upon whom the senior corporate manager or senior officer reasonably relies in making the certification.

requirements.description: Submit all annual certifications to the Commission via email to DEbrief@ftc.gov or by overnight courier to Associate Director for Enforcement, with subject line beginning "In re D-Link Systems, Inc., FTC File No. X170030"
requirements.quoted_text: B. Unless otherwise directed by a Commission representative in writing, submit all annual certifications to the Commission pursuant to this Order via email to DEbrief@ftc.gov or by overnight courier (not the U.S. Postal Service) to Associate Director for Enforcement, Bureau of Consumer Protection, Federal Trade Commission, 600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20580. The subject line must begin, “In re D-Link Systems, Inc., FTC File No. X170030.”

provisions.title: Specific Conduct Provisions
provisions.summary: Defendant must stop selling or distributing the IP Camera set-up wizard software containing representations in Exhibit C, and must notify registered consumers to update their device firmware within 60 days.

requirements.description: Defendant shall no longer sell, distribute, or host on its website the IP Camera set-up wizard software containing the representations shown in Exhibit C for any Covered Devices
requirements.quoted_text: A. Defendant shall no longer sell, distribute, or host on its website the IP Camera set- up wizard software containing the representations shown in Exhibit C attached hereto for any Covered Devices.

requirements.description: Within 60 days of the effective date of this Order, provide clear and conspicuous notice to all consumers who registered their Covered Devices, through the communication channel(s) the consumer chose at the time of registration, containing instructions for updating said device with the latest firmware update
requirements.quoted_text: B. Within 60 days of the effective date of this Order, provide clear and conspicuous notice to all consumers who registered their Covered Devices, through the communication channel(s) the consumer chose at the time of registration, containing instructions for updating said device with the latest firmware update.

provisions.title: Order Acknowledgments
provisions.summary: Defendant must obtain and submit acknowledgments of receipt of this Order from itself and relevant personnel, and deliver copies to all current and future responsible personnel.

requirements.description: Within 7 days of entry of this Order, submit to the Commission an acknowledgment of receipt of this Order sworn under penalty of perjury
requirements.quoted_text: A. Defendant, within 7 days of entry of this Order, must submit to the Commission an acknowledgment of receipt of this Order sworn under penalty of perjury.

requirements.description: For three years after entry of this Order, deliver a copy of this Order to: (1) all principals, officers, directors, and LLC managers and members; (2) all employees having managerial responsibilities for the security of Covered Devices and all agents and representatives who participate in the security of Covered Devices; and (3) any business entity resulting from any change in structure. Delivery must occur within 7 days of entry of this Order for current personnel and before others assume their responsibilities
requirements.quoted_text:
B. For three years after entry of this Order, Defendant must deliver a copy of this Order to: (1) all principals, officers, directors, and LLC managers and members;
(2) all employees having managerial responsibilities for the security of Covered Devices and all agents [PROPOSED]
requirements.deadline: 7 days for current personnel; before assuming responsibilities for others

requirements.description: From each individual or entity to which a copy of this Order was delivered, obtain within 30 days a signed and dated acknowledgment of receipt of this Order
requirements.quoted_text: C. From each individual or entity to which a Defendant delivered a copy of this Order, that Defendant must obtain, within 30 days, a signed and dated acknowledgment of receipt of this Order.

provisions.title: Compliance Reporting
provisions.summary: Defendant must file a compliance report on January 31, 2020 and timely notices for any changes in structure or bankruptcy filings, submitted sworn under penalty of perjury.

requirements.description: On January 31, 2020, submit a compliance report sworn under penalty of perjury identifying contact information, all businesses, activities, compliance status for each Order section, and copies of Order Acknowledgments

For ten (10) years after entry of this Order, submit a compliance notice sworn under penalty of perjury within 14 days of any change in designated point of contact or in the structure of Defendant or any entity that Defendant has any ownership interest in or controls directly or indirectly that may affect compliance obligations under this Order
requirements.quoted_text: B. For ten (10) years after entry of this Order, Defendant must submit a compliance notice, sworn under penalty of perjury, within 14 days of any change in the following: (a) any [PROPOSED]

requirements.description: Submit to the Commission notice of the filing of any bankruptcy petition, insolvency proceeding, or similar proceeding by or against Defendant within 14 days of its filing
requirements.quoted_text: C. Defendant must submit to the Commission notice of the filing of any bankruptcy petition, insolvency proceeding, or similar proceeding by or against such Defendant within 14 9 days of its filing.

requirements.description: Any submission required to be sworn under penalty of perjury must be true and accurate and comply with 28 U.S.C. § 1746
requirements.quoted_text: D. Any submission to the Commission required by this Order to be sworn under penalty of perjury must be true and accurate and comply with 28 U.S.C. § 1746, such as by concluding: “I declare under penalty of perjury under the laws of the United States of America that the foregoing is true and correct. Executed on: _____” and supplying the date, signatory’s full name, title (if applicable), and signature.

requirements.description: Unless otherwise directed in writing, all submissions must be emailed to DEbrief@ftc.gov or sent by overnight courier to Associate Director for Enforcement, Bureau of Consumer Protection, FTC, 600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20580, with subject line beginning "FTC v. D-Link Systems, Inc., X170030"
requirements.quoted_text: E. Unless otherwise directed by a Commission representative in writing, all submissions to the Commission pursuant to this Order must be emailed to DEbrief@ftc.gov or sent by overnight courier (not the U.S. Postal Service) to: Associate Director for Enforcement, Bureau of Consumer Protection, Federal Trade Commission, 600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20580. The subject line must begin: FTC v. D-Link Systems, Inc., X170030.

provisions.title: Recordkeeping
provisions.summary: Defendant must create and retain specified records for ten years after entry of the Order (each retained for 5 years), including accounting records, personnel records, consumer complaints, compliance documents, and marketing materials.

requirements.description: Create certain records for ten (10) years after entry of the Order, and retain each such record for 5 years, specifically: (A) accounting records showing revenues from all goods or services sold; (B) personnel records showing name, addresses, telephone numbers, job title, dates of service, and reason for termination; (C) records of all consumer complaints and refund requests; (D) all records necessary to demonstrate full compliance with each provision of this Order, including all submissions to the Commission; and (E) a copy of each unique advertisement or other marketing material making a representation subject to this Order
requirements.quoted_text:
IT IS FURTHER ORDERED that Defendant must create certain records for ten (10) years after entry of the Order, and retain each such record for 5 years. Specifically, Defendant must create and retain the following records:

A. accounting records showing the revenues from all goods or services sold; [PROPOSED]

C. records of all consumer complaints and refund requests, whether received directly or indirectly, such as through a third party, concerning the subject matter of the Order;

D. all records necessary to demonstrate full compliance with each provision of this Order, including all submissions to the Commission; and E. a copy of each unique advertisement or other marketing material by Defendant making a representation subject to this Order.

provisions.title: Compliance Monitoring
provisions.summary: The FTC has the right to monitor Defendant's compliance through document requests, depositions, discovery, and interviews, with Defendant required to respond within 14 days of written requests.

requirements.description: Within 14 days of receipt of a written request from a representative of the Commission, Defendant must submit additional compliance reports or other requested information (sworn under penalty of perjury), appear for depositions, and produce documents for inspection and copying; the Commission is also authorized to obtain discovery using Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 29, 30, 31, 33, 34, 36, 45, and 69
requirements.quoted_text: A. Within 14 days of receipt of a written request from a representative of the Commission, Defendant must: submit additional compliance reports or other requested information, which must be sworn under penalty of perjury; appear for depositions; and produce documents for inspection and copying. The Commission is also authorized to obtain discovery, without further leave of court, using any of the procedures prescribed by Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 29, 30 (including telephonic depositions), 31, 33, 34, 36, 45, and 69. Provided, however, that Defendant, after attempting to resolve a dispute without court action and for good cause shown, may file a motion with this Court seeking an order for one or more of the protections set forth in Rule 26(c).

requirements.description: For matters concerning this Order, the Commission is authorized to communicate directly with Defendant; Defendant must permit representatives of the Commission to interview any employee or other affiliated person who has agreed to such an interview, with counsel present if desired
requirements.quoted_text: B. For matters concerning this Order, the Commission is authorized to communicate directly with Defendant, Defendant must permit representatives of the Commission to interview [PROPOSED]

requirements.description: The Commission may use all other lawful means, including posing as consumers, suppliers, or other individuals or entities, without the necessity of identification or prior notice; nothing in this Order limits the Commission's lawful use of compulsory process under Sections 9 and 20 of the FTC Act
requirements.quoted_text: C. The Commission may use all other lawful means, including posing, through its representatives, as consumers, suppliers, or other individuals or entities, to Defendant or any individual or entity affiliated with Defendant, without the necessity of identification or prior notice. Nothing in this Order limits the Commission’s lawful use of compulsory process, pursuant to Sections 9 and 20 of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C. §§ 49, 57b-1, nor does it limit Defendant’s ability to assert any and all objections, defenses, rights, or privileges available to it, as to any such process.

provisions.title: Retention of Jurisdiction
provisions.summary: The Court retains jurisdiction over this matter for the purposes of construction, modification, and enforcement of this Order.

requirements.description: The Court retains jurisdiction over this matter for the purposes of construction, modification, and enforcement of this Order

duration.termination_text: IT IS ORDERED that Defendant shall, for a period of twenty (20) years after entry of this Order, continue with or establish and implement, and maintain, a comprehensive software security program ("Software Security Program") that is designed to provide protection for the security of its Covered Devices, unless Defendant ceases to market, distribute, or sell any Covered Devices.

takeaway_brief: D-Link marketed routers and IP cameras as secure while leaving them vulnerable to hard-coded credentials, command injection flaws, and backdoors.
takeaway_full: D-Link failed to take reasonable steps to secure the software in its consumer routers and IP cameras against well-known vulnerabilities—including hard-coded credentials, command injection flaws, and backdoors—and exposed its private code-signing key publicly for approximately six months, while simultaneously marketing its products as secure and protected against unauthorized access. The FTC required D-Link to implement a comprehensive software security program, undergo third-party security assessments, provide annual certifications, and comply with specific conduct requirements.
